The Virgin Mary's monthly commemoration recurs on this day, following immediately upon the feast of Ganna, as if the Church cannot bear to celebrate the birth of the Son without immediately turning again to the face of the Mother. Mary's presence on this day of the Shepherds is theologically precise: she is the one who pondered all these things and kept them in her heart, even as the shepherds departed glorifying and praising God. The Mother is the keeper of the mystery, the contemplative center within the narrative of the Nativity; while others proclaim and marvel and return to their daily lives, she remains in the cave of Bethlehem with her Child, holding in her heart the fullness of what she has received.

The theological seal of this twenty-sixth day of Tahsas is the mystery of witness flowing outward from the center. The shepherds receive the light at the manger and carry it outward to the city; Joshua leads the people from the wilderness to the land of promise; Mary holds the center of the mystery in contemplative silence. Together they describe the movement of the Church in the wake of the Nativity: from the contemplative reception of the mystery at its source, outward in proclamation to the surrounding world, and ever and always returning to the central figure — the Mother and her Child — who remain the inexhaustible fountain of all Christian life.

27.title: 27 Tahsas — The Massacre of the Innocents & The Assembly
27.narrative:
The twenty-seventh day of Tahsas commemorates the Holy Innocents, those infant boys of Bethlehem and its environs who were slaughtered by the command of King Herod the Great in his murderous attempt to destroy the Christ Child. The Church has always honored these children as martyrs — indeed, as the first martyrs of the new dispensation, the flowers of the Church before the Church had fully blossomed, who died for Christ without the capacity to understand what they died for, giving their lives in the place of the One who would later give His life for all. Their number, traditionally reckoned at fourteen thousand in the Eastern tradition though the historical reality was certainly more modest, expresses the theological enormity of what was done: the Incarnate Lord arrived among His own people and the world's response was the murder of children.

The monthly commemoration of the Crucifixion of Our Lord falls on this day, and its juxtaposition with the Massacre of the Innocents is theologically devastating in its precision. The infants of Bethlehem die that the Christ Child might live; the Christ Child lives that He might grow to die on the cross for all the children of Adam. The blood of the Innocents at the beginning of the Lord's earthly life and the blood of the Lord on the cross at the end of it form two banks of the same river: the river of redemptive suffering that flows through the entire economy of salvation. Herod sheds the blood of the innocent thinking to prevent salvation; God permits the shedding of innocent blood knowing that it will become itself a participation in the great act of salvation that the Innocent One will accomplish on Calvary.

Saint Stephen the Protomartyr, whose monthly commemoration appears again on this day, stands as the adult counterpart of the Holy Innocents: as they died without knowledge of the one for whom they died, he died in full knowledge and full freedom, seeing the heavens opened and the Son of Man standing at the right hand of God. The Coptic and Ethiopian churches have always placed Stephen close to the Nativity season, for his martyrdom is the first human echo of the divine self-offering that the Child of Bethlehem came to accomplish. The crown that his name promises was the first crown given after the Nativity; the innocents' crowns were given at the same time, by the same sword of Herodian wickedness.

The theological seal of this twenty-seventh day of Tahsas is the mystery of innocent suffering as participation in the redemptive mystery. The Innocents suffer innocently, as the Innocent One will suffer on the cross; Stephen suffers willingly, as the willing victim of Calvary; the monthly Crucifixion commemoration draws both into the eternal act of the self-giving God. The Incarnation that Tahsas celebrates is not a comfortable mystery of warmth and light in isolation: it is a mystery of light entering darkness, of divine love entering the world's violence, of the innocent Lamb coming among wolves — and being, in the end, more powerful than all the wolves in their fury.

28.title: 28 Tahsas — The Holy Patriarchs & The Assembly
28.narrative:
The twenty-eighth day of Tahsas honors the Holy Patriarchs — Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, and the company of the righteous ancestors of the Lord — those great progenitors of faith through whom God prepared the lineage and the people from which the Messiah would come. Abraham, the father of faith, who left his homeland at the divine word, who offered his son Isaac upon the mountain of Moriah in an act that the entire patristic tradition reads as the supreme type of the Father offering His only-begotten Son; Isaac, the child of the impossible promise, the son of laughter, who carried the wood of his own sacrifice upon his back up the mountain just as the second Isaac would carry the wood of the cross; Jacob, the wrestler who prevailed with the Angel of the Lord and received the name Israel — 'he who strives with God' — and who on his deathbed gathered his twelve sons about him and prophesied the coming of the Shiloh to whom the obedience of the nations would belong. These patriarchs are the human roots of the divine tree whose fruit is the Child of Bethlehem.

Saint Theodore the Sanctified, the fourth-century Egyptian monastic saint who was a disciple of Pachomius and eventually the leader of the Pachomian federation of monasteries, brings to this day the witness of organized communal life as the earthly image of the heavenly community of the patriarchs. Theodore, whose name in the Greek means 'gift of God,' was a man of extraordinary personal holiness and administrative wisdom; he guided thousands of monks through the desert disciplines that formed them into a community of prayer and mutual service. His life within the monastic tradition was itself a living exegesis of the patriarchal narratives: as Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob formed the family of God's covenant, so Theodore and his brothers formed the monastic family of the new covenant, whose common life was itself a proclamation of the Kingdom.

Saint Nicanor the Deacon, one of the Seven Deacons chosen by the apostles in Jerusalem and mentioned in the Acts of the Apostles alongside Stephen and Philip, completes this day's assembly. Nicanor, whose name in the Greek means 'victor' or 'conqueror,' served the early Jerusalem community with the same dedication as his better-known companions; the tradition records that two thousand of his flock were martyred alongside him on the same day as the stoning of Stephen, giving this obscure deacon a martyrological significance far beyond what his brief mention in the canonical text might suggest.

The theological seal of this twenty-eighth day of Tahsas is the mystery of ancestral faith fulfilled in the Nativity. The patriarchs are not merely historical figures but living members of the communion of saints who — as the Epistle to the Hebrews declares — are present as a cloud of witnesses to the race that the living Church is running. On this day, so close to the feast of Ganna, the Church gathers the ancestors of the faith into the assembly of the Nativity: Abraham and Isaac and Jacob stand in the cave of Bethlehem alongside the shepherds and the angels and the wise men, for it is their Seed who lies in the manger, the fulfilment of all their striving and all their hope.

29.title: 29 Tahsas — The Triple Feast & The Assembly
29.narrative:
The twenty-ninth day of Tahsas is designated in the Ethiopian liturgical tradition as the Triple Feast — the commemoration of three of the greatest mysteries simultaneously observed in a single solemn celebration. These three mysteries, whose particular configuration in the Ethiopian calendar reflects the ancient tradition that the Church of Alexandria maintained, include the commemoration of the Lord's Baptism, the Transfiguration, and the Resurrection in a telescoped theological meditation that situates the Nativity of Ganna within the full arc of the Lord's earthly life and glorification. The birth of the twenty-fifth is thus interpreted by the twenty-ninth: the Child who was born in the cave of Bethlehem is the same Lord who descended into the waters of the Jordan, who was transfigured upon the mountain in dazzling light, and who rose from the dead on the third day. The feast of the Incarnation cannot be understood apart from its culmination in the Paschal mystery.

The Twenty-Four Elders of Heaven, whose commemoration falls on this day, are the heavenly assembly described in the Apocalypse of John: twenty-four elders clothed in white garments with golden crowns upon their heads who worship the Lamb day and night, casting their crowns before the throne and singing 'Worthy is the Lamb that was slain.' The Ethiopian Church has given these Elders a particularly important liturgical role; they appear in iconography, in hymnody, and in the architecture of sacred space as the permanent celestial choir whose unceasing praise frames all earthly worship. On this day, so close to the end of Tahsas, their worship gathers up the entire month's meditation on the Incarnation and offers it back to the God who gave it.

The Holy Children of Babylon — Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego, and with them Daniel himself in the broader tradition — are commemorated on this day, those young men of Israel who were cast into the burning fiery furnace by King Nebuchadnezzar for their refusal to bow before the golden image of false divinity, and who walked unharmed in the midst of the flames accompanied by a fourth figure 'like unto a Son of God.' The fathers have always read this fourth figure as an appearance of the pre-incarnate Word, the eternal Son who was destined to take flesh in Tahsas; and the furnace has been read as a type of the Virgin's womb, from which the Son of God emerged without corrupting the seal of her virginity, as He walked in the furnace without singeing even the hair of His companions.

The theological seal of this twenty-ninth day of Tahsas is the mystery of the Nativity encompassed by the totality of salvation. The Triple Feast declares that Bethlehem is not the end of the story but its beginning; the Twenty-Four Elders declare that the story's end is the unceasing worship of the Lamb; the Holy Children declare that the God who enters the furnace of human suffering — the furnace of the Virgin's womb, the furnace of the cross, the furnace of death — emerges victorious and makes His companions victorious with Him. The month of the Incarnation concludes by placing its central mystery within the widest possible horizon: from the creation of the world to the worship of heaven, all things converge upon the Child of Bethlehem.

30.title: 30 Tahsas — St. John Chrysostom & The Assembly
30.narrative:
The thirtieth and final day of Tahsas honors Saint John Chrysostom, the golden-mouthed Archbishop of Constantinople, whose surname Chrysostomos — 'golden mouth' in the Greek — he received posthumously from a Church that recognized in his preaching a quality of eloquence and theological depth rarely equalled in the history of Christian proclamation. John was born in Antioch around the year three hundred and forty-nine, formed in the rhetorical tradition of the ancient world and in the ascetic tradition of the Syrian desert, and was elevated to the archiepiscopal throne of the new Rome against his own wishes. His preaching on the Epistles of Paul, his homilies on the Gospels, his treatise On the Priesthood, and his liturgy — still prayed in the Eastern churches each Sunday — constitute a treasury of Christian theology and spirituality that the Church has never ceased to draw upon. He died in exile, broken in body but unbroken in spirit, praying 'Glory to God for all things' — a doxology that has become, in the Eastern tradition, the summary of the Christian attitude toward all the vicissitudes of mortal life.

Saint Anysia of Thessalonica, a virgin martyr of the Diocletianic persecution, adds to this closing day of Tahsas the witness of a woman who devoted her considerable inherited wealth to the service of the poor, the ransoming of prisoners, and the care of the sick — until she was encountered by a pagan soldier who attempted to drag her to the imperial sacrifices and killed her when she made the sign of the cross and called upon the name of Jesus. Her name Anysia in the Greek is connected with the word for 'completion' or 'fulfillment' — and indeed her death completed the program of her life: she who had given away everything earthly gave at last her very life, and in so doing fulfilled the logic of her entire vocation.

Saint Melania the Younger, the Roman noblewoman of the late fourth and early fifth centuries who with her husband Pinian renounced one of the greatest fortunes of the ancient world to embrace the ascetic life, completes the assembly of this final day of Tahsas. Melania's name in the Greek means 'black' or 'dark' — perhaps a family name of Semitic origin — yet her life became a radiance of charity that illumined the social darkness of a world in which wealth was hoarded while multitudes starved. She and Pinian liberated eight thousand slaves, funded monasteries in North Africa, Palestine, and Constantinople, and spent their final years in Jerusalem, where Melania founded a monastery on the Mount of Olives and died there in the year four hundred and thirty-nine, venerated by all who knew her as a living icon of the evangelical poverty that the Lord of Bethlehem had embraced in His birth.

The theological seal of this thirtieth and final day of Tahsas is the mystery of the month's completion in the spirit of the golden doxology. John Chrysostom closes his life with 'Glory to God for all things' — and this doxology is the proper ending of the month of the Incarnation, which has moved from the groaning of Adam in his exile to the cry of the angels over Bethlehem, from the darkness of prophetic expectation to the blinding light of Ganna, from the witness of the martyrs in their suffering to the praise of the Twenty-Four Elders before the throne of the Lamb. The entire month of Tahsas is itself a doxology: thirty days of the Church's voice rising in praise for the mystery that surpasses all mysteries, the love that surpasses all loves, the gift that surpasses all gifts — the Word made flesh, who dwelt among us, full of grace and truth.
